---
title: "The validation harness"
subtitle: "T6 ¬∑ Phase T6.3 ¬∑ `va-harness`"
---

::: {.callout-note}
**Status: partial.** The comparison machinery is real and tested; the golden data isn't there
yet. See "Limitations" below for exactly why, and [the roadmap](../../roadmap.md)'s T6.3 section
for the full change log.
:::

## Goal

Turn "the simulator produced some numbers" into "the simulator produced the *right* numbers":
solve a circuit through the real pipeline, diff it against a committed reference, and report a
clear pass/fail ‚Äî the one thing that actually earns a ‚úÖ on the ladder-rung table, per
`CLAUDE.md` ¬ß7.

## Where it fits

`va-harness` sits at the very end of the pipeline `CLAUDE.md` ¬ß2 draws, downstream of
everything else:

```
Verilog-A source          circuit netlist
       ‚îÇ                         ‚îÇ
  [va-frontend] ‚îÄ‚îÄIR‚îÄ‚îÄ‚ñ∫ [va-codegen]      [va-netlist]
                              ‚îÇ                ‚îÇ
                        ModelInstance (Interface Œ≤)
                              ‚îÇ                ‚îÇ
                              ‚ñº                ‚ñº
                         [va-core] ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚ñ∫ [va-cli] ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚ñ∫ [va-harness] ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚ñ∫ golden/
                                                          ‚îÇ
                                                     (ngspice, offline,
                                                      as the oracle)
```

It doesn't add a new capability to the simulator itself ‚Äî it drives the *existing* `va-cli`
pipeline and checks the answer, the same way a test suite doesn't make the code faster, it makes
you trust that it's correct.

## The idea

Three pieces, matching `docs/validation.md` ¬ß7:

1. **A metric** ‚Äî how far apart are "what we computed" and "what's golden"? `va_harness::metrics`
   has two: `max_relative_error` (DC ‚Äî the worst relative deviation across every point) and
   `rms_error` (transient ‚Äî root-mean-square over an already-aligned waveform).
2. **A golden format** ‚Äî a file format to *store* that reference. `va_harness::golden` has two so
   far: `GoldenDc` (one operating point) and `GoldenSweep` (a `.dc` sweep, one `GoldenDc`-shaped
   row per swept value).
3. **A driver** ‚Äî something that actually runs the pipeline, builds a comparable result, and
   applies the metric. `va_harness::dc` does this for both golden shapes; `cargo xtask validate`
   is the command-line entry point that ties it to a curated list of circuits.

ngspice is the oracle throughout (`CLAUDE.md` ¬ß7): we are not building on it, only checking
against it. That one sentence turns out to matter a lot for how honest this phase can be about
its own progress ‚Äî see Limitations.

### The DC metric, precisely

```
rel = max_i |got_i - ref_i| / max(|ref_i|, floor)
```

`floor` (`1e-12`) exists purely so a golden value of exactly `0` ‚Äî a diode's leakage current at
`V=0`, say ‚Äî doesn't turn a negligible absolute difference into a division-by-zero blowup. It's
a division guard, not a "this is a small error" threshold in its own right; a genuinely large
absolute difference against a near-zero reference still divides out to something huge and fails
the check, exactly as it should.

### The golden formats

A `GoldenDc` (`.golden`) is as plain as it gets:

```text
# golden/divider.golden
in 1
mid 0.5
```

A `GoldenSweep` adds one header line naming the swept source and the node order, then one row
per point:

```text
# golden/diode_iv.golden
V1 in
0 0
0.1 0.1
```

Both round-trip exactly through `f64`'s `Display` (Rust's shortest-round-trippable-decimal
formatting, not a truncated one) ‚Äî `render()` then `parse()` always reproduces the original
value bit-for-bit, which matters for a format whose entire job is being a trustworthy reference.

## The code

`va_cli::load` ‚Äî parses a netlist and, if given, compiles a `.va` model through the real
frontend ‚Üí codegen pipeline. `run_sim` (the CLI's own entry point) already did this inline;
it's now its own `pub fn` so `va-harness` can reuse it instead of re-implementing netlist parsing
or shelling out to the CLI binary and re-parsing stdout:

```rust
pub fn load(netlist: &str, model: Option<&str>) -> Result<(Netlist, Vec<Module>)>
```

`va_cli::solve_dc` / `solve_dc_sweep` ‚Äî likewise made `pub`, unchanged otherwise. They already
existed to serve `run_sim`'s own DC/sweep reporting.

`va_harness::dc::run_dc` ‚Äî the glue: load, solve, package as a `GoldenDc`.

```rust
pub fn run_dc(circuit: &str, model: Option<&str>) -> Result<GoldenDc, HarnessError> {
    let (net, compiled) = va_cli::load(circuit, model)...?;
    let op = va_cli::solve_dc(&net, &compiled)...?;
    Ok(GoldenDc::from_operating_point(&net.node_order, &op.x))
}
```

`va_harness::dc::compare_dc` ‚Äî diff two `GoldenDc`s. Node order must match exactly first ‚Äî a
silent value-by-position diff across differently-ordered nodes would compare unrelated
quantities and could pass by accident:

```rust
pub fn compare_dc(got: &GoldenDc, golden: &GoldenDc) -> Result<Verdict, HarnessError> {
    if got.node_order != golden.node_order {
        return Err(HarnessError::NodeOrderMismatch { .. });
    }
    let error = metrics::max_relative_error(&got.values, &golden.values)?;
    Ok(Verdict::new(error, tol::DC_REL))
}
```

`run_dc_sweep`/`compare_dc_sweep` do the identical thing for a `.dc` sweep, flattening every
point's node voltages into one long series before handing it to the same `max_relative_error`.

`cargo xtask validate` walks two small, explicit tables (`DC_CIRCUITS`, `SWEEP_CIRCUITS`) ‚Äî one
entry per circuit this project currently knows how to golden-check ‚Äî and for each: if
`golden/<name>.golden` exists, run + compare + report `PASS`/`FAIL`; if it doesn't, report `skip`
rather than failing the build. An empty `golden/` (this project's actual state ‚Äî see
Limitations) is a legitimate "nothing captured yet," not an error.

## It works

The comparison logic itself ‚Äî metrics, golden parse/render round-trips, node-order/length
mismatch rejection ‚Äî is unit-tested without needing any external tool:

```console
$ cargo test -p va-harness --lib
running 28 tests
test golden::tests::sweep_render_round_trips_through_parse ... ok
test metrics::tests::max_relative_error_floor_guards_a_near_zero_reference ... ok
test dc::tests::run_dc_solves_the_divider ... ok
test dc::tests::run_dc_sweep_solves_the_diode_iv_curve ... ok
test dc::tests::compare_dc_sweep_passes_for_an_identical_reference ... ok
...
test result: ok. 28 passed; 0 failed; 0 ignored; 0 measured; 0 filtered out; finished in 0.00s
```

`cargo xtask validate` against this project's actual (empty) `golden/`:

```console
$ cargo run -q -p xtask -- validate
[xtask] validate: running va-harness over the model zoo vs golden/ ‚Ä¶
[xtask]   skip circuits/divider.net: no golden reference at .../golden/divider.golden
[xtask]   skip circuits/mos_dc.net: no golden reference at .../golden/mos_dc.golden
[xtask]   skip circuits/diode_iv.net: no golden reference at .../golden/diode_iv.golden
[xtask] validate: 0 checked, 0 failed, 3 skipped (no golden)
```

Exit code `0` ‚Äî skipping isn't failing. Now drop in a golden file by hand (this is exactly
`divider.net`'s already-known analytic answer, `V(in)=1`, `V(mid)=1¬∑R2/(R1+R2)=0.5` ‚Äî not a
fabricated value, just typed in manually instead of generated by `gen-golden`, which is why this
worked example doesn't commit the file):

```console
$ printf "in 1\nmid 0.5\n" > golden/divider.golden
$ cargo run -q -p xtask -- validate
[xtask]   PASS circuits/divider.net: error=0.000e0 (tol 1e-4)
[xtask]   skip circuits/mos_dc.net: no golden reference at ...
[xtask]   skip circuits/diode_iv.net: no golden reference at ...
[xtask] validate: 1 checked, 0 failed, 2 skipped (no golden)
$ rm golden/divider.golden   # this file is not committed ‚Äî see Limitations
```

A deliberately-wrong golden value (`mid 0.9` instead of `0.5`) reports `FAIL` and a non-zero exit
‚Äî confirmed the same way, not just asserted in prose.

## Limitations

**No `golden/*.golden` file is committed, on purpose.** `cargo xtask gen-golden` ‚Äî the command
that's supposed to produce one, by actually running ngspice ‚Äî is not implemented: this
development environment has no ngspice installed (`gen-golden` reports exactly that, rather than
silently no-op'ing), and even with ngspice present, this project still needs to write and verify
a `circuits/*.net` ‚Üí ngspice-deck translator, which hasn't happened. The worked example above
types a golden value in by hand from an *already independently known* analytic answer ‚Äî that's
fine for demonstrating the comparison machinery, but it would **not** be fine to commit as a real
golden reference, because the entire point of `CLAUDE.md` ¬ß7's "ngspice is the oracle" rule is
that golden data comes from an *independent* simulator, not from this project's own hand-derived
math. A `golden/` file with a hand-computed value in it would quietly stop being a check on
correctness and start being a check on arithmetic consistency with itself.

**DC only.** `rms_error` (the transient metric) is implemented and unit-tested, but nothing
feeds it a real waveform yet ‚Äî that needs a `GoldenWaveform` format and a "shared-timebase
resample" step (two different transient runs won't land on the same timepoints unless one is
resampled onto the other's), neither written yet. `.tran` circuits (`rc_step.net`,
`rectifier.net`) aren't in any `xtask validate` table for this reason.

**A per-rung/convergence-fraction dashboard** (¬ß7's fourth metric, "fraction of zoo circuits
that reach a solution") doesn't exist ‚Äî `validate`'s own pass/fail/skip tally is the closest
thing today.

## What's next

1. Write and verify a `circuits/*.net` ‚Üí ngspice-deck translator, on a machine that actually has
   ngspice installed ‚Äî the real blocker for `gen-golden`, and for every ladder rung's golden gate
   ever going green.
2. `GoldenWaveform` + `rms_error`'s shared-timebase resample, to bring `.tran` circuits into
   `xtask validate`.
3. Once real golden data exists for even one rung, that rung is the project's first genuinely
   ‚úÖ-complete ladder milestone ‚Äî worth its own celebration, and its own worked example replacing
   this tutorial's hand-typed stand-in.
